original_hypothesis.title: Burns Accumulate address(0) in Holders Array Causing Permanent Distribution DoS
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: Every burn operation (burn/burnFrom) triggers _beforeTokenTransfer with to=address(0). Since balanceOf(address(0)) is always 0, the check `this.balanceOf(to) != 0` is always false, causing `holders.push(address(0))` on every burn. The _afterTokenTransfer hook only attempts to remove `from` (the burner), never address(0). Over time, the holders array grows unboundedly with address(0) entries. distributeToAllHolders() processes each entry, eventually exceeding block gas limits. Even partial distribute() calls waste gas iterating over phantom entries.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Burns are a public function via ERC20Burnable. Any token holder can burn 1 wei repeatedly to inflate the holders array. The address(0) entries are never cleaned up because _afterTokenTransfer only checks `from`, and no other function removes them.

proof_of_signal.pattern: During burn: to=address(0), balanceOf(address(0))=0 always, so holders.push(address(0)) executes on every burn

preconditions: Attacker holds any amount of LiquidInfrastructureERC20 tokens
burn() is not blocked (within MinDistributionPeriod, not locked)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: to=address(0) during burns always evaluates balanceOf(address(0))==0, triggering holders.push(address(0))
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: No guard against pushing address(0); no cleanup mechanism for phantom entries
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Missing `if (to == address(0)) return;` check before exists check

analysis_questions: How many burns are needed to make distributeToAllHolders() exceed block gas limit?
Can partial distribute() work around the inflated array, or is gas cost per entry too high?
Is there any mechanism to remove address(0) entries from holders?
validation_requirements: Burn tokens N times and verify holders.length increases by N with address(0) entries
Calculate gas per holder entry in distribute() to determine DoS threshold
Verify distributeToAllHolders() reverts with OOG after sufficient burns

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker burns 1 wei per transaction thousands of times. Each burn pushes address(0) to holders. Once holders array contains enough entries (~10K+), distributeToAllHolders() exceeds block gas limit. Even partial distribute() calls spend significant gas on phantom entries. Protocol becomes unable to complete distributions efficiently, potentially permanently.
prerequisites: Token balance (even 1 wei sufficient for many burns)

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Permanent DoS on distribution mechanism; all accumulated revenue becomes inaccessible
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All token holders cannot receive distributions
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: No on-chain recovery — no function to clean holders array

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No tests for holders array state after burns

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: ~50K gas per holder entry in distribute(); block gas limit / 50K = max processable holders

validation_result.mechanism: address(0) accumulation in holders array via burn
validation_result.impact_summary: Every burn operation pushes address(0) into the holders array. Since this entry is never cleaned up during burns, the array grows unboundedly, eventually causing distributeToAllHolders() to exceed block gas limits and permanently DoS the distribution mechanism.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The _beforeTokenTransfer hook is called by OpenZeppelin's ERC20._burn with from=burner and to=address(0). The function correctly skips the allowlist check for address(0) via `if (!(to == address(0)))`, but then unconditionally evaluates `bool exists = (this.balanceOf(to) != 0)` where to=address(0). Since balanceOf(address(0)) is always 0 in standard ERC20 (burns deduct from the burner, they don't credit address(0)), `exists` is always false, causing `holders.push(address(0))` on every single burn. The _afterTokenTransfer hook only attempts to remove `from` (the burner) from holders when their balance reaches 0 — it never touches the address(0) entries. While mints do trigger partial cleanup of address(0) entries (since _afterTokenTransfer checks from=address(0) during mints), the swap-and-pop loop without break means only some entries are removed per mint, and burns can easily outpace mints. Any token holder can burn 1 wei repeatedly to inflate the array with minimal cost.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof_of_signal correctly identifies the vulnerable code in _beforeTokenTransfer. The balanceOf(address(0)) check on the `to` parameter always evaluates to 0 during burns, causing holders.push(address(0)) to execute. The _afterTokenTransfer function only processes `from` (the burner), never address(0) entries added during burns. This is a clear code defect — the missing guard `if (to == address(0)) return;` or `if (to != address(0))` before the exists check.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: During burns, to=address(0). The balanceOf(address(0)) is always 0, so exists=false, causing holders.push(address(0)) on every burn. No guard prevents address(0) from being pushed.

During burns, from=burner. This function only attempts to remove the burner from holders if their balance is 0. It never removes the address(0) entry pushed by _beforeTokenTransfer during the same burn operation.

distribute() iterates over every entry in holders. Each address(0) entry wastes gas on the isApprovedHolder check and loop overhead. With enough entries, distributeToAllHolders() exceeds block gas limit.

calling_sequence: 4. _beforeTokenTransfer: LockedForDistribution check passes, allowlist check skipped for address(0), _beforeMintOrBurn() called (to==address(0)), then this.balanceOf(address(0)) returns 0, exists=false, holders.push(address(0)) executes
5. ERC20._burn updates balances (deducts from msg.sender, does NOT credit address(0))
7. _afterTokenTransfer checks balanceOf(msg.sender): if still > 0, does nothing; if 0, removes msg.sender from holders. Never removes address(0).
8. address(0) entry persists in holders array permanently (unless a mint happens to partially clean up)
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The critical path is in _beforeTokenTransfer where `bool exists = (this.balanceOf(to) != 0)` evaluates to false when to=address(0) (because ERC20 burns subtract from the sender's balance, never credit address(0)). This causes `holders.push(address(0))` to execute unconditionally on every burn. The _afterTokenTransfer hook only processes `from` (the burner), meaning the address(0) entry is never removed during a burn operation. Over repeated burns, the holders array accumulates address(0) entries unboundedly, eventually causing distributeToAllHolders() to exceed the block gas limit.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: Deployment script at contract-deployer.ts deploys with MinDistributionPeriod=10, which is a non-zero value. The deployment does not affect this vulnerability since it is independent of configuration.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Attack is straightforward and realistic: 1) Attacker holds any amount of LiquidInfrastructureERC20 tokens (even 1 wei is sufficient for many burns). 2) Attacker calls burn(1) repeatedly in separate transactions. Each call pushes address(0) to holders array. 3) After ~10K+ burns (depending on block gas limit and gas per holder iteration), distributeToAllHolders() exceeds block gas limit. 4) Even partial distribute() calls must iterate over phantom address(0) entries, wasting gas. 5) No on-chain mechanism exists to clean up the holders array (aside from partial cleanup during mints, which is insufficient if burns outpace mints). The cost is minimal — just gas for burn transactions of 1 wei each.
validation_result.impact_assessment: CRITICAL. The holders array grows by one address(0) entry per burn. With enough burns, distributeToAllHolders() becomes permanently unusable (OOG). Partial distribute() calls waste gas on phantom entries. Since distributions are the core mechanism for revenue delivery to token holders, this effectively DoSes the entire protocol. All accumulated revenue becomes inaccessible to holders. There is no on-chain recovery mechanism — no function exists to remove arbitrary entries from the holders array.
recommended_mitigations: Add a guard in _beforeTokenTransfer to skip holder tracking for address(0): move the holders.push logic inside a `if (to != address(0))` block
Alternatively, add `if (to == address(0)) return;` after the _beforeMintOrBurn() call but before the exists check
Consider using a mapping (address => bool) for O(1) exists checks instead of relying on balanceOf, to also prevent duplicate entries from zero-value transfers
Fix the _afterTokenTransfer loop to add a `break` after the swap-and-pop removal to prevent skipping elements
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy LiquidInfrastructureERC20 with MinDistributionPeriod=1. 2. Approve holder A and mint 1000 tokens to A. 3. Call burn(1) from A 100 times. 4. Read holders.length — expect 101 (1 for A + 100 address(0) entries). 5. Verify holders[1] through holders[100] are all address(0). 6. Attempt distributeToAllHolders() and measure gas consumption scaling linearly with burn count. 7. Repeat burns until distributeToAllHolders() exceeds block gas limit.
uncertainties: Exact number of burns needed for full DoS depends on block gas limit and gas cost per holder iteration in distribute()
Mints partially clean up address(0) entries via _afterTokenTransfer (though the missing-break bug limits effectiveness), so the net growth rate depends on mint/burn ratio
additional_testing_needed: Measure exact gas cost per holder entry in distribute() to calculate DoS threshold
Test interaction between burn-induced address(0) entries and mint-induced partial cleanup
Verify that the _afterTokenTransfer missing-break bug prevents complete cleanup of address(0) entries during mints
